title: SonarQube Scoped Organization tokens should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: SonarQube scoped organization token detected in source code. SonarQube scoped organization tokens (sqa_ prefix) provide organization-level API access to SonarQube and SonarCloud platforms, enabling comprehensive quality management operations across all projects within an organization. These tokens grant extensive permissions including: managing organization settings and billing, creating and configuring projects across the organization, managing quality profiles and quality gates organization-wide, accessing and modifying security hotspots and vulnerabilities across all projects, managing organization members and their permissions, configuring webhooks and integrations at organization level, accessing code analysis results and metrics for all projects, managing organization-level portfolios and applications, controlling ALM integrations (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, Azure DevOps), managing security policies and compliance rules, accessing audit logs and usage analytics, and controlling organization-level authentication and authorization settings. Exposing SonarQube organization tokens enables attackers to: access sensitive code analysis data including security vulnerabilities and code quality metrics across all projects, modify quality gates allowing vulnerable code to pass checks, disable security rules and vulnerability detection, access source code visibility and analysis results, manipulate project configurations and analysis settings, create backdoor projects for malicious code, access organization billing and subscription information, modify organization member permissions and roles, inject malicious webhooks for data exfiltration, tamper with compliance and governance policies, access integration credentials for ALM platforms, and consume API quotas disrupting quality analysis operations. This is a critical security vulnerability requiring immediate remediation. Take immediate action: revoke the exposed organization token immediately from SonarQube/SonarCloud organization settings (Organization → Administration → Security → Tokens), generate a new organization token with minimal necessary scopes and permissions, update all applications, CI/CD pipelines, and integrations with the new token, review SonarQube audit logs for unauthorized access or suspicious activity patterns, check which projects and repositories the exposed token had access to, audit all recent changes to quality gates, quality profiles, and security configurations, verify no security rules or quality gates were disabled or weakened, review all webhook configurations for unauthorized data exfiltration endpoints, implement principle of least privilege for token permissions (use project-level tokens when organization-level access isn't required), store SonarQube tokens in secure secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GitHub Secrets), use environment variables with restricted access for CI/CD pipeline integration, never commit SonarQube tokens to version control or expose in configuration files, implement secret scanning in version control systems and CI/CD pipelines, use SonarQube service accounts with appropriate permission scopes for automation, rotate SonarQube tokens regularly (every 90 days recommended as per security best practices), enable SAML/SSO and enforce MFA for SonarQube organization accounts, monitor SonarQube API usage for anomalies and unusual access patterns, implement IP whitelisting for API access when possible, document all SonarQube tokens in a secure credential inventory, use project-specific tokens instead of organization tokens when possible to limit blast radius, educate development teams about proper token handling and the sensitivity of code quality/security scanning credentials, integrate SonarQube token detection in pre-commit hooks and CI/CD security gates, and regularly audit token permissions and access patterns for security compliance.
